steps.title: Identify what the experiment showed
steps.content: Tanks with watermilfoil released lower methane than tanks with duckweed or algae. The blank asks what this finding suggests. A valid inference must follow directly from that result — lower methane in the watermilfoil tanks — without introducing anything the experiment didn't test.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — the presence of some kinds of underwater plants like watermilfoil helps prevent methane from escaping shallow lakes and ponds.: Directly matches the finding: watermilfoil tanks had less methane, suggesting watermilfoil helps prevent methane escape. ✓
  • Choice B — shallow lakes and ponds release more methane than deeper bodies of water because shallow bodies of water usually have more plants than deep bodies of water do.: The experiment never compared shallow to deep bodies of water. No basis for this claim. ✗
  • Choice C — shallow lakes and ponds are more likely to contain algae than to contain either watermilfoil or duckweed.: The experiment compared methane levels, not the frequency of plant types in the wild. This goes beyond the data. ✗
  • Choice D — having a mix of algae, underwater plants, and floating plants is the best way to reduce the amount of methane in shallow lakes and ponds.: No tank had a mix of plants, so the experiment gives no evidence about mixed-plant environments. ✗
That matches Choice A — the presence of some kinds of underwater plants like watermilfoil helps prevent methane from escaping shallow lakes and ponds..
